author: Don Miguel Ruiz
theme: Four codes for personal freedom based on ancient Toltec wisdom
verses: 1. From childhood, we are taught what to believe, how to behave, what is good and what is bad. This is called domestication.
2. We create a book of law in our minds that judges us constantly. We never measure up to our own standards.
3. The Four Agreements offer freedom from self-limiting beliefs.
4. FIRST AGREEMENT: Be impeccable with your word.
5. Speak with integrity. Say only what you mean. Use words to create, not to destroy.
6. Avoid using words against yourself. Avoid gossip. Your word has power—use it wisely.
7. Impeccable means 'without sin.' Sin against yourself is using your word against yourself.
8. SECOND AGREEMENT: Don't take anything personally.
9. Nothing others do is because of you. It is a projection of their own reality.
10. When you are immune to the opinions of others, you will not be the victim of needless suffering.
11. Don't even take your own self-critical thoughts personally. They too are projections.
12. THIRD AGREEMENT: Don't make assumptions.
13. Find the courage to ask questions and communicate clearly.
14. Assumptions are based on incomplete information. They create unnecessary drama and misunderstanding.
15. Clear communication is the antidote to assumption. Ask. Clarify. Confirm.
16. FOURTH AGREEMENT: Always do your best.
17. Your best changes from moment to moment. When you are tired, your best is different than when you are rested.
18. Do your best, and you will avoid self-judgment and regret.
19. Don't do more than your best—it drains energy. Don't do less—it causes frustration.
20. These four agreements create personal liberation. Practice them daily.
21. When you break an agreement, do not judge yourself. Simply begin again.
22. Freedom comes not from perfection, but from consistent practice.
23. The dream of the planet can change when we change ourselves.
24. Be impeccable. Don't take things personally. Don't assume. Do your best.
25. This is the path to personal freedom.

book_title: The Tao Te Ching - The Way and Its Power
author: Lao Tzu
theme: Living in harmony with the Tao - the fundamental principle underlying existence
verses: 1. The Tao that can be spoken is not the eternal Tao. The name that can be named is not the eternal name.
2. The nameless is the origin of heaven and earth. The named is the mother of all things.
3. Free from desire, you observe the mystery. Filled with desire, you see only manifestations.
4. All things arise together. I watch their return. They flourish, then return to the source.
5. Return to the source is called stillness. This is the way of nature.
6. The highest good is like water. Water gives life to all things and does not strive.
7. It flows to low places that others reject. Therefore it is like the Tao.
8. In dwelling, live close to the ground. In thinking, keep to the simple. In conflict, be fair.
9. Practice not-doing, and everything will fall into place.
10. The Master does not compete, and therefore no one can compete with him.
11. Thirty spokes share the wheel's hub; it is the center hole that makes it useful.
12. Clay is shaped into a vessel; it is the emptiness within that makes it useful.
13. Walls form a room; it is the space within that makes it useful.
14. Being is what we work with. Non-being is what we use.
15. Do your work, then step back. The only path to serenity.
16. The soft overcomes the hard. The slow overcomes the fast. The flexible outlasts the rigid.
17. A good leader is one whose existence is barely known. The worst is one who is hated.
18. When the Master governs, the people barely notice. Work is done, and they say: 'We did it ourselves.'
19. In pursuit of learning, every day something is acquired. In pursuit of Tao, every day something is dropped.
20. Less and less do you need to force things, until finally you arrive at non-action.
21. When nothing is done, nothing is left undone.
22. The Master has three treasures: compassion, frugality, and humility.
23. From compassion comes courage. From frugality comes generosity. From humility comes leadership.
24. He who knows others is wise. He who knows himself is enlightened.
25. Be content with what you have. Rejoice in the way things are.
26. When you realize there is nothing lacking, the whole world belongs to you.
27. True words are not eloquent. Eloquent words are not true.
28. Wise people do not need to prove their point. Those who prove their point are not wise.
29. The Master gives without keeping score, and is fulfilled precisely because of this.
30. The Tao nourishes all things. It gives birth and does not possess. It acts without expectation. It guides without dominating.
31. This is called the mysterious virtue.

book_title: The Dhammapada - Sayings of the Buddha
theme: The path of righteousness - core teachings of Siddhartha Gautama
verses: 1. All that we are arises with our thoughts. With our thoughts, we make the world.
2. Speak or act with an impure mind, and trouble will follow you as the wheel follows the ox that draws the cart.
3. Speak or act with a pure mind, and happiness will follow you as your shadow, unshakable.
4. 'He abused me, he struck me, he defeated me, he robbed me.' Those who harbor such thoughts do not still their hatred.
5. Hatred is never ended by hatred. Hatred is only ended by love. This is the eternal law.
6. The wise harm no one. They are masters of their bodies and find refuge in the deathless.
7. As rain penetrates an ill-thatched house, so passion penetrates an undeveloped mind.
8. As rain does not penetrate a well-thatched house, so passion does not penetrate a well-developed mind.
9. Those who mistake the unessential for the essential and the essential for the unessential never arrive at the essential.
10. Those who know the essential to be essential and the unessential to be unessential arrive at the essential.
11. Irrigators guide water. Fletchers shape arrows. Carpenters bend wood. The wise shape themselves.
12. As a solid rock is not shaken by the wind, so the wise are not shaken by praise or blame.
13. Health is the greatest gift. Contentment is the greatest wealth. Faithfulness is the best relationship.
14. Let no one deceive another. Let no one despise anyone anywhere. Let no one wish harm on another.
15. Conquer anger with kindness. Conquer evil with good. Conquer the miser with generosity. Conquer the liar with truth.
16. An idea that is developed and put into action is more important than an idea that exists only as an idea.
17. Do not dwell in the past. Do not dream of the future. Concentrate the mind on the present moment.
18. The mind is everything. What you think, you become.
19. Peace comes from within. Do not seek it without.
20. No one saves us but ourselves. No one can and no one may. We ourselves must walk the path.
21. Even death is not to be feared by one who has lived wisely.
22. The root of suffering is attachment. Let go, and find peace.
23. In the end, only three things matter: how much you loved, how gently you lived, and how gracefully you let go.
24. You yourself must strive. The Buddhas only point the way.
25. Work out your own salvation. Do not depend on others.

author: Michael A. Singer
theme: The journey beyond yourself - freeing the inner self
verses: 1. There is nothing more important to true growth than realizing you are not the voice of the mind—you are the one who hears it.
2. The voice talks, but you are the one who is aware of the voice talking.
3. This is a great moment in your spiritual evolution: recognizing you are not your thoughts.
4. In there, behind all that internal dialogue, is you—the one who is aware of that dialogue.
5. You are the witness. You are the observer. You are consciousness itself.
6. The mind creates problems. Then it tries to solve them. This creates suffering.
7. When you learn to stay open regardless of circumstances, you find lasting peace.
8. When you close, you close to life. When you open, you open to everything.
9. Pain is not the problem. Resistance to pain is the problem.
10. Energy flows through you constantly. When you relax and open, you feel unlimited energy.
11. When you tighten and resist, you block the flow. Fatigue and depression follow.
12. Let go of the tendency to define yourself based on external circumstances.
13. Your inner state should not depend on outer events.
14. Fear is caused by blockages. Love is what flows when the blockages are removed.
15. Do not protect your psyche. Expose it. Let it experience. Let it grow.
16. The spiritual journey is about ceasing to defend yourself.
17. When you stop defending, you start growing.
18. Death is not your enemy. Clinging to life is what causes suffering.
19. Live each moment fully. Do not postpone living.
20. The highest spiritual path is life itself. Everything that happens is a teaching.
21. If you lean into every experience, you will grow beyond all limitations.
22. The seat of the witness is the only place you will ever find peace.
23. Go beyond yourself. Untether your soul. Let it soar.
24. You are not your body. You are not your mind. You are the one who experiences both.
25. This is your birthright: freedom. Claim it.

book_title: The Power of Now - Key Teachings
author: Eckhart Tolle
theme: Spiritual enlightenment through present moment awareness
verses: 1. Realize deeply that the present moment is all you ever have.
2. The past and future are illusions. Only Now is real.
3. Your life situation exists in time. Your life exists in the Now.
4. The moment you place your attention fully in the Now, you feel a presence, a stillness, a peace.
5. The ego draws its identity from the past and future. It cannot survive in the present moment.
6. Identification with the mind creates an opaque screen of concepts, labels, images, and judgments.
7. This screen blocks all true relationship with everything and everyone.
8. Accept—then act. Whatever the present moment contains, accept it as if you had chosen it.
9. Always work with it, not against it. Make it your friend and ally, not your enemy.
10. This will miraculously transform your whole life.
11. The pain-body is accumulated emotional pain from the past.
12. It feeds on more pain. It wants you to think painful thoughts and create painful drama.
13. When you recognize the pain-body in yourself, you can begin to disidentify from it.
14. When you are present, when your attention is fully in the Now, the pain-body cannot feed.
15. Enlightenment is simply your natural state of felt oneness with Being.
16. It is not attainment. It is recognition of what you already are.
17. Stop looking outside for scraps of pleasure or fulfillment. There is a vast reservoir of Being within you.
18. Joy does not come from what you do. It flows into what you do from deep within you.
19. The primary cause of unhappiness is never the situation, but your thoughts about it.
20. Be aware of the thoughts you are thinking. Separate them from the situation.
21. Surrender is the simple but profound wisdom of yielding to rather than opposing the flow of life.
22. To offer no resistance to life is to be in a state of grace, ease, and lightness.
23. This state is no longer dependent on things being a certain way.
24. Die to the past every moment. You don't need it.
25. The Power of Now is here. Step into it.

book_title: The Metta Sutta - The Discourse on Loving-Kindness
theme: The practice and power of universal loving-kindness
verses: 1. This is what should be done by one who is skilled in goodness.
2. Let them be able and upright, straightforward and gentle in speech.
3. Humble and not conceited, contented and easily satisfied.
4. Unburdened with duties and frugal in their ways. Peaceful and calm, and wise and skillful.
5. Not proud and demanding in nature. Let them not do the slightest thing that the wise would later reprove.
6. Wishing: In gladness and in safety, may all beings be at ease.
7. Whatever living beings there may be—whether they are weak or strong, omitting none.
8. The great or the mighty, medium, short, or small. The seen and the unseen.
9. Those living near and far away. Those born and to be born.
10. May all beings be at ease!
11. Let none deceive another, or despise any being in any state.
12. Let none through anger or ill-will wish harm upon another.
13. Even as a mother protects with her life her child, her only child.
14. So with a boundless heart should one cherish all living beings.
15. Radiating kindness over the entire world: spreading upwards to the skies.
16. And downwards to the depths, outwards and unbounded.
17. Freed from hatred and ill-will.
18. Whether standing or walking, seated or lying down.
19. Free from drowsiness, one should sustain this recollection.
20. This is said to be the sublime abiding.
21. By not holding to fixed views, the pure-hearted one.
22. Having clarity of vision, being freed from all sense desires.
23. Is not born again into this world.
24. May all beings be happy. May all beings be peaceful. May all beings be free.
25. This is the practice of loving-kindness.

book_title: The Kalama Sutta - The Buddha's Charter of Free Inquiry
theme: Question everything - the Buddha's instruction on independent investigation
verses: 1. Do not accept anything on mere hearsay.
2. Do not accept anything by mere tradition.
3. Do not accept anything on account of rumors.
4. Do not accept anything just because it accords with your scriptures.
5. Do not accept anything by mere supposition.
6. Do not accept anything by mere inference.
7. Do not accept anything by merely considering the appearances.
8. Do not accept anything merely because it agrees with your preconceived notions.
9. Do not accept anything merely because it seems acceptable.
10. Do not accept anything simply because your teacher says so.
11. But when you know for yourselves: 'These things are unwholesome, these things are blameworthy.'
12. 'These things are censured by the wise. These things, when undertaken and practiced, lead to harm and suffering.'
13. Then you should abandon them.
14. And when you know for yourselves: 'These things are wholesome, these things are blameless.'
15. 'These things are praised by the wise. These things, when undertaken and practiced, lead to welfare and happiness.'
16. Then you should enter upon and abide in them.
17. This is the standard of the wise: investigate and discern for yourself.
18. Freedom comes through clear understanding, not blind obedience.
19. The Buddha did not say: 'Believe because I say so.'
20. He said: 'Come and see. Test it for yourself.'
21. This is the spirit of inquiry that leads to liberation.
22. Trust your own experience. Develop your own wisdom.
23. The path is walked alone, though teachers point the way.
24. Question everything—including this teaching.
25. Only what survives honest inquiry is worth keeping.
